summary: Yegge recounts his unexpectedly viral Foo Camp talk about porting Ruby on Rails to JavaScript running on the JVM via Mozilla Rhino. He explains Google's pragmatic language policy (C++, Java, Python, JavaScript only) and why this constraint, combined with his project's JVM requirements, led him to choose Rhino over Jython. The post details how they extended JavaScript with Ruby/Python-like methods and began a 20% project to add JavaScript 1.7 features to Rhino. He emphasizes that Google's clean codebase and engineering discipline enable this kind of experimentation within well-defined boundaries.
key_points: Google limits production languages to C++, Java, Python, and JavaScript to build language expertise, reduce interoperability complexity, and enable easy team transfers
Rhino (server-side JavaScript on JVM) was chosen over Jython because Jython stagnated since 2001 while Rhino has momentum, a real spec, and Sun's endorsement in Java 6
They extended JavaScript with Ruby and Python built-in methods by leveraging Rhino's ability to define non-enumerable properties
The Rails port covers ActionView, ActionController, and Railties, done manually with Emacs keyboard macros rather than code generators
Google's engineering culture requires design documents for anything over a week of work, resulting in comprehensive internal documentation for every significant piece of code
money_quotes: I am privileged to work at Google. It's a privilege. It's something that I have no expectations about.
I'm nobody at Google. I'm a very comfy, very small fish in a sea full of large, toothy sea creatures.
The thing is, Google's decision to limit the number of languages allowed for production work is actually pretty smart.
Programming languages have a core of standard functionality that everyone knows, followed by a long tail of murky semantics that become increasingly difficult to figure out.
I've never seen anything like it before, to be honest. I don't think you can get that kind of software-engineering discipline without doing it right from the start.
Ports are kinda boring when you get right down to it.
Rails is its own most succinct implementation, with very few exceptions.
key_insight: Smart language constraints at scale aren't limiting—they enable experimentation by building deep expertise and reducing organizational friction.
